text: The surgeon performs a standard ileocolic resection using three laparoscopic ports.
The surgical setup includes three ports located at the umbilicus, right lower quadrant, and suprapubic regions.
The surgeon tents the ileocolic pedicle and begins a medial to lateral dissection, carefully separating the pedicle from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon uses a ligature device for dissection of inflamed tissues, achieving improved hemostasis during the procedure.
The surgeon enters the retroperitoneal plane and maneuvers around the ileocolic pedicle, continuing the dissection process.
The surgeon exposes large lymph nodes within the ileocolic pedicle during the dissection.
The surgeon completes the dissection of the ileocolic pedicle using a ligature device, ensuring hemostasis in the inflamed tissue area.
The surgeon continues with a medial to lateral dissection, progressing through the retroperitoneal plane around the ileocolic pedicle.
The surgeon marks the small bowel mesenteric transection area, noting the thickened appearance of the bowel and mesentery in this region.
The surgeon uses ICG mode to visualize the ureteral stent, ensuring a safe and efficient dissection without encroaching upon the right ureter.
The surgeon examines the hepatic flexure, planning the lateral mobilization while noting that some medial dissection remains to be completed.

The surgeon completes the dissection of the colon mesentery, moving towards the ascending colon and hepatic flexure. The lateral dissection is then initiated, focusing on mobilizing the hepatic flexure.
The surgeon continues the dissection, meeting the medial plane and mobilizing the right colon around the hepatic flexure towards the iliocolic region.

The surgeon adjusts the angle of the camera to approach the dissection from an inferior perspective, utilizing blunt dissection to assist in the process. Granulation tissue from a previous perforation is visible in the surgical field.

The surgeon has detached the colon from the retroperitoneum and is now addressing the residual attachments to the small bowel mesentery. The procedure is being performed in ICG mode, ensuring the right ureter is not at risk during this dissection.
The surgeon frees the remaining small bowel mesentery up to the planned proximal margin. The mesentery is completely taken, and the surgeon checks perfusion at both margins.

The surgeon marks the area with clips for visibility under white light. The stapling is performed from the suprapubic port in preparation for exteriorizing through a Pfannenstiel incision, ensuring the stapler aligns correctly with the target tissue.
The surgeon divides the colon using a surgical stapler with purple loads, followed by the division of the small bowel.
The surgeon prepares to line up the bowel segments for an intracorporeal anastomosis, positioning them within the abdominal cavity.

The surgeon aligns the bowel with a stay suture tethered to the abdominal wall and staples through the suprapubic port site using a white load stapler. The common channel is then closed with a three OB lock suture, running it in a lumbered fashion to secure the closure.
The surgeon demonstrates the baseball stitch technique, advancing the suture towards the far corner of the anastomosis site.
The surgeon completes the closure of the anastomosis with a stitch, ensuring the corner is securely closed.
The surgeon performs perfusion checks and adjusts the closure by limbering the suture back down, ensuring the integrity of the surgical site.
The surgeon uses a dunking stitch to secure the anastomosis, which appears well-perfused, ensuring proper blood flow through the newly connected bowel segments.
The surgeon sutures around and below the original corner of the anastomosis, ensuring complete closure of the surgical site.
The surgeon uses a suspending stitch to elevate the anastomosis, allowing for clear visualization underneath with the assistance of two working arms.
The surgeon completes the procedure by exteriorizing the specimen from the pelvis through the suprapubic port site. A small incision is made using an Alexis wound protector.
